title: Family: Talking About the People Closest to You
objectives: Name close family members in Spanish.
Use mi, mis, tu, tus, su, and sus with family words.
Talk about a spouse, partner, children, parents, siblings, grandparents, and in-laws.
Use tengo, tiene, vive, and se llama in simple family sentences.
Describe your family naturally in a short conversation with Carlos.

vocabulary.tip: Use la familia for close family and, depending on context, extended family too.
A natural phrase for starting a family description.
Use mi esposa for my wife.
Use mi esposo for my husband.
Pareja can mean partner and is broader than esposo or esposa.
Hijos can mean sons or children in general.
Use el hijo for a son.
Use la hija for a daughter.
Madre is neutral or formal. Mamá is warmer in everyday speech.
Mamá is warm and informal. Use mi mamá, not usually la mamá, when speaking personally.
Padre is neutral or formal. Papá is warmer in everyday speech.
Papá is warm and informal. Use mi papá when talking about your dad.
Los padres means parents. Do not use parientes for parents.
Use hermano for one brother.
Use hermana for one sister.
Los hermanos can mean brothers or siblings in a mixed or general group.
Abuelito is an affectionate form of abuelo.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Mi abuelo vive con mi tia.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My grandfather lives with my aunt.

vocabulary.tip: Abuelita is an affectionate form of abuela.
Los abuelos can mean grandparents or grandfathers depending on context.
Suegro means father-in-law, not son-in-law.
Suegra means mother-in-law.
Often means parents-in-law specifically.
Cuñado means brother-in-law. The ñ sounds like ny in canyon.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Mi cuñado trabaja en una escuela.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My brother-in-law works at a school.

vocabulary.tip: Cuñada means sister-in-law. The ñ sounds like ny in canyon.
Tio means uncle. In Spain it can also be casual slang for guy, but not in this lesson.
Tia means aunt and is also used warmly in some families.
Use primo for a male cousin.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My male cousin has two children.

vocabulary.tip: Use prima for a female cousin.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My female cousin lives in Canada.

vocabulary.english: is named / his or her name is
vocabulary.tip: Use se llama to say another person's name.
Use tengo to talk about your family.
Use tiene for he has, she has, or formal you have.
Use vive to say where one family member lives.

familyPhraseVariants.whenUsed: Esposa is clear and safe everywhere. Mujer can mean wife in Spain and other regions, but esposa is better for beginners.
Esposo is clear everywhere. Marido is also natural for husband, especially in Spain and many Latin American contexts.
Pareja is useful when partner is more accurate or when you do not want to specify wife or husband.
Hijos can mean children in general, not only sons.
Padres is neutral. Mamá and papá are warmer and more personal.
Use tu with one family member. Use tus with plural family members.
Use hermanos for siblings when gender is mixed or not important.

familyPhraseVariants.mexicanSpanishVersion: Mi suegra es muy buena onda.
familyPhraseVariants.whenUsed: Amable is safe everywhere. Buena onda and maja are casual regional options.
Both are natural. Tengo una familia pequeña focuses on what you have.
Use se llama for names of relatives.

grammar.explanation: Family sentences often use possessives plus a family noun: mi esposa, mis hijos, tu hermano, sus padres. Use tengo for I have, tiene for he or she has, and se llama to say a person's name.

notes.title: Mi / mis
notes.text: Use mi before one family member and mis before more than one: Mi hija vive aqui. = My daughter lives here. Mis hijos viven aqui. = My children live here. Practice: say one sentence with mi and one with mis. Watch out: do not say mi padres.

notes.title: Tu / tus
notes.text: Use tu before one family member and tus before more than one: Tu hermano se llama Luis. = Your brother is named Luis. Tus hermanos viven cerca. = Your siblings live nearby. Practice: ask Carlos about one relative, then more than one. Watch out: tu is also the informal you in other contexts, but here it means your.

notes.title: Su / sus
notes.text: Su means his, her, your formal, or their before one family member. Sus is the plural version: Su madre vive aqui. = His/her mother lives here. Sus padres viven aqui. = His/her parents live here. Practice: describe Carlos' family with su or sus. Watch out: context tells whose family you mean.

notes.title: Tengo vs tiene
notes.text: Tengo means I have. Tiene means he has, she has, or formal you have: Tengo dos hijos. = I have two children. Carlos tiene una hermana. = Carlos has one sister. Practice: say what you have, then say what Carlos has. Watch out: do not say yo tiene.

notes.title: Se llama
notes.text: Use se llama to say someone is named: Mi esposa se llama Ana. = My wife is named Ana. Practice: name two relatives with se llama. Watch out: do not say se llama es.

notes.title: Singular and plural family words
notes.text: One person uses a singular word: el hijo, la hermana. More than one uses plural: los hijos, las hermanas, los hermanos. Los hermanos can mean siblings. Practice: change mi hermano to mis hermanos. Watch out: hermano is one brother; hermanos is plural.

notes.title: Gender with family nouns
notes.text: Many family words change for male and female: hijo/hija, hermano/hermana, suegro/suegra, primo/prima. Mi hijo se llama Leo. = My son is named Leo. Mi hija se llama Sofia. = My daughter is named Sofia. Practice: say one male relative and one female relative. Watch out: the ending changes the relationship.

notes.title: Hay
notes.text: Hay means there is or there are: En mi familia hay cinco personas. = In my family there are five people. Practice: say how many people there are in your family. Watch out: hay does not change for singular or plural.

examples.spanish: Tengo dos hijos y mis padres viven cerca.
examples.english: I have two children and my parents live nearby.

grammar.practicePrompt: Tell Carlos about your family using at least five family words, two possessive words, one name with se llama, and one sentence with tengo or tiene.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, Carlos. Mi esposa se llama Ana.
lines.english: Hi, Carlos. My wife is named Ana.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Nice to meet her. What is Ana like?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: No, I do not have children.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Nice to meet you, sir and ma'am.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: How nice. Do you see them often?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Mi familia es pequeña. Tengo una esposa, dos hijos y una hermana.
lines.english: My family is small. I have a wife, two children, and one sister.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Perfecto, Tom. Tu familia suena muy bonita.
lines.english: Perfect, Tom. Your family sounds very nice.

pronunciation.focus: Family word rhythm, silent h, ñ, and plural endings.
tips: Familia has three smooth parts: fa-mi-lia. Keep each vowel clear.
The h is silent in hijo, hija, hermano, and hermana.
The ñ in cuñado and cuñada sounds like ny in canyon.
Suegro and suegra begin with a smooth swe sound.
In mis hijos, pronounce the final s in mis and remember the h in hijos is silent.

items.note: Keep the rhythm smooth and the vowels clear.
The h is silent; start with the vowel sound.
The h is silent; keep hija short and clear.
The h is silent; do not add an English h sound.
The h is silent; link la hermana smoothly.
Keep sue-gra as two clear parts.
The ñ sounds like ny in canyon.
The ñ sounds like ny in canyon.
Final s in mis links into hijos; the h is silent.

listeningPhrases.note: Listen for mi familia as one smooth phrase.
Listen for tengo plus a number.
Listen for hija and se llama.
The h in hijo is silent.
Listen for mis, the plural possessive.

listeningPhrases.spanish: En mi familia hay cinco personas.
listeningPhrases.english: In my family there are five people.
listeningPhrases.note: Hay can mean there is or there are.

culture.title: Family talk is warm, personal, and practical
culture.text: Mamá and papá sound warmer and more personal than madre and padre, while madre and padre can feel neutral, formal, or descriptive. Familia can mean the people in your home or your extended family, depending on context. Family terms are often used warmly, and affectionate forms like abuelita or abuelito can show closeness. Suegro and suegra are parents-in-law; cuñado and cuñada are siblings-in-law. Pareja is useful because it can mean partner more broadly than esposo or esposa.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers talk about family
notes: Hijos often means children, not only sons.
Hermanos can mean siblings when the group is mixed or unspecified.
Padres means parents; parientes means relatives.
People often say mi mamá and mi papá in warm everyday conversation.
Mi pareja is common when partner is more accurate or more comfortable than esposo or esposa.
For in-laws, suegros usually means parents-in-law, not all relatives by marriage.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using el with mama or papa in a personal sentence.
commonMistakes.correction: Say mi mamá or mi papá when talking about your own mom or dad.
commonMistakes.explanation: La mamá and el papá can work in some contexts, but mi mamá and mi papá sound more natural for your family.

commonMistakes.correction: Use hijos for your children and ninos for children in general.
commonMistakes.explanation: Tengo dos hijos means I have two children. Dos ninos just means two kids.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying mi esposa se llama es Ana.
commonMistakes.correction: Say mi esposa se llama Ana.
commonMistakes.explanation: Se llama already means is named.

commonMistakes.correction: Say mis padres, mis hijos, and tus hermanos.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mi and tu are singular. Mis and tus are plural.

commonMistakes.correction: Use tengo for I have and tiene for he/she has.
commonMistakes.explanation: Tengo una hermana. Carlos tiene una hermana.

commonMistakes.correction: Use hermanos for more than one sibling.
commonMistakes.explanation: Hermano is singular. Hermanos is plural and can mean siblings.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing suegro with yerno or nuera.
commonMistakes.correction: Suegro is father-in-law. Suegra is mother-in-law.
commonMistakes.explanation: Yerno means son-in-law and nuera means daughter-in-law; those are different relationships.

review.summary: You can now talk about close family members, say names with se llama, use possessives like mi and mis, and answer simple questions about who is in your family.
essentials: Immediate family: mi esposa, mi esposo, mi pareja, mis hijos.
Parents and siblings: mi mamá, mi papá, mis padres, mis hermanos.
Extended family: mis abuelos, mi tio, mi tia, mi primo, mi prima.
In-laws: mi suegro, mi suegra, mis suegros, mi cuñado, mi cuñada.
Core verbs: tengo, tiene, se llama, vive.
selfCheck: Can I say who is in my immediate family?
Can I introduce one family member by name with se llama?
Can I use mi for one person and mis for more than one?
Can I ask Carlos if he has siblings?
Can I say where one family member lives?
review.nextStep: Now that you can introduce the people closest to you, the next lesson will help you talk about ages, times, dates, and schedules.

quiz.prompt: Choose the Spanish for my family.
What does se llama mean in Mi hija se llama Sofia?
Choose the correct possessive: _____ padres viven cerca.
What is the best response to Tienes hermanos?

options: Mi esposa se llama es Ana.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence means I have two children?
Choose the correct plural form for my siblings.
Complete: En mi familia _____ cinco personas.
Translate: Mi madre vive en Canada.
Carlos asks: Como se llama tu hija? Choose the best answer.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Say who is in your immediate family.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: En mi familia hay una esposa y dos hijos.
Mi esposa se llama Ana. / Mi pareja se llama Ana.
Tengo dos hijos. / No tengo hijos.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Name two relatives and say where they live.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Mi madre vive en Canada y mi hermano vive en Mexico.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Tell Carlos about your family using at least five family words and two possessive words.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Mi familia es pequeña. Tengo una esposa, dos hijos y una hermana. Mis padres viven cerca.

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Tell Carlos about your family using at least five family words, two possessive words, one name using se llama, and one sentence using tengo or tiene.
successCriteria: Use at least five family words, such as esposa, hijos, padres, hermano, or suegros.
Use at least two possessive words, such as mi, mis, tu, tus, su, or sus.
Use se llama to name at least one family member.
Use tengo or tiene in one complete sentence.
Answer one follow-up question from Carlos.
